coredata.dc:title: A new indium-phenanthroline based complex as a fluorescence sensor: Synthesis, characterization and detection of multiple analytes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ya-Qian
Ma, Wan-Lin
Li, Wen-Ze
Lan, Ya-Lin
Liu, Yi
Zhao, Ya-Nan
Luan, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Chemical contaminants have posed a growing danger to environmental security and human health in recent years, causing considerable alarm. Herein, a new indium-based complex was synthesized by solvothermal method and using 1,10-phenanthroline (phen), namely, [(CH3)2NH2]+·[In(phen)Cl4]− (1). The crystal structure of the complex 1 was determined by single crystal X-ray diffraction (SCXRD). Moreover, elemental analysis (EA), Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis and other structural characterization were carried out in detail. Thermal and solvent stabilities were investigated thoroughly. The liquid and solid fluorescence spectra had been also explored to discuss the influence of synthesis conditions on crystal configuration and the relationship between the complex and fluorescence properties. Moreover, complex 1 has good fluorescence properties and can be served as an outstanding candidate for the multi-response sensing of Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr2O7 2− ions and nitrobenzene (NB). Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) and fluorescence quenching mechanism were studied in detail.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Indium complex
1,10-phenanthroline
Thermal and solvent stabilities
Fluorescence sensor
Selectivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PXRD patterns, FTIR spectra and TGA of complex 1
Thermal and solvent stabilities of complex 1
Multi-response fluorescence sensing of metal ions
Multi-response fluorescence sensing of toxic organic solvents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Coordination environment of complex 1; (b) View of binuclear structure of complex 1; (c) Typical SEM image of complex 1; (d–f) SEM–EDS mapping images and corresponding element mapping of complex 1.
PXRD patterns of complex 1 treated with different solvents (a) and temperatures (b).
Fluorescence spectra of the complex 1, 1,10-phenanthroline ligand and 1,4-dioxane solution.

(a) Histogram of fluorescence intensities of complex 1 after soaking with different metal ions; (b) Fluorescence spectra of complex 1 changed with the increase of Co2+ ion; (c) KSV plot of complex 1 for sensing of Co2+ ion; (d) Quenching fluorescence intensity of complex 1 by Co2+ ion.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of complex 1 after soaking with different anions; (b) Fluorescence spectra of complex 1 changed with the increase of Cr2O7 2− anion; (c) KSV plot of complex 1 for sensing of Cr2O7 2− anion.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of complex 1 after soaking with different organic solvents; (b) Fluorescence spectra of complex 1 changed with the increase of NB; (c) KSV plot of complex 1 for sensing of NB.
UV–vis adsorption spectra of different analytes and the emission spectrum of complex 1.
Structural characteristics of In-based complexes with different ligands.
List of typical complex utilized for the sensing of Co2+ ion.
List of typical complex utilized for the sensing of Cr2O7 2− anion.
List of typical complex utilized for the sensing of NB.

head.ce:title: A new indium-phenanthroline based complex as a fluorescence sensor: Synthesis, characterization and detection of multiple analytes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Shenyang University of Chemical Technology, Shenyang 110142, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Science, Shenyang University of Chemical Technology, Shenyang 110142, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Test Center, Shenyang University of Chemical Technology, Shenyang 110142, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cAnalysis and Test Center, Shenyang University of Chemical Technology, Shenyang 110142, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical contaminants have posed a growing danger to environmental security and human health in recent years, causing considerable alarm. Herein, a new indium-based complex was synthesized by solvothermal method and using 1,10-phenanthroline (phen), namely, [(CH3)2NH2]+·[In(phen)Cl4]− (1). The crystal structure of the complex 1 was determined by single crystal X-ray diffraction (SCXRD). Moreover, elemental analysis (EA), Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis and other structural characterization were carried out in detail. Thermal and solvent stabilities were investigated thoroughly. The liquid and solid fluorescence spectra had been also explored to discuss the influence of synthesis conditions on crystal configuration and the relationship between the complex and fluorescence properties. Moreover, complex 1 has good fluorescence properties and can be served as an outstanding candidate for the multi-response sensing of Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr2O7 2− ions and nitrobenzene (NB). Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) and fluorescence quenching mechanism were studied in detail.

A new indium-based complex was synthesized by solvothermal method, which possesses good solution stability and thermal stability. Moreover, complex 1 is a rare example of multi-response In-based chemical sensors for high sensitivity and selective detection of metal ion, oxygen-containing anion and toxic organic solvent. The mechanism of the sensing process shows that the synergistic effect of electron transfer through the fluorescent ligand and FRET mechanism leads to fluorescence quenching.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, water pollution caused by harmful and toxic organic pollutants has become increasingly serious and is a serious problem worldwide [1–3]. Heavy metals, suspended solids and toxic organics are common in wastewater [4–9]. A large number of detecting techniques have been reported so far for the remediation of wastewater samples such as cyclic voltammetry, gas chromatography, and Raman spectroscopy [10–14]. However, these methods are time-consuming and costly, which limits their large-scale application [15–20]. In recent years, fluorescence sensing technology has been widely concerned for its excellent performance such as high operability, low cost and fast response. At present, a number of the fluorescence sensing materials include nanomaterials [21], synthetic organic materials [22] and inorganic compounds [23] had been explored. However, these fluorescent sensing materials are often soluble in solvents and thus difficult to recycle and reuse [24–26]. In addition, some solid insoluble fluorescence sensing materials, such as silicon compounds with low specific surface area and tiny porosity, make it difficult to have a significant number of binding active sites [27]. Moreover, in practical applications, small molecular organic fluorescence materials may also have poor stability and low sensitivity [28]. Therefore, it is still a major challenge to design and synthesize a fluorescence sensing material with low limit of detection (LOD), high selectivity, high sensitivity, simple operation and fast response speed.

Coordination complexes are extremely interesting not just because of their diverse properties and highly customizable structure, but also for the reason of their incredible applications in sensing, catalysis, gas storage, separation, and drug delivery [29]. Fluorescent coordination complexes are commonly used as optical sensors for detecting explosives [30], metal ions [31], liquids [32], and biomolecules [23]. Compared with transition metal/rare earth-based complexes, indium-based complexes have three obvious advantages: (i) Compared with rare earth metals, indium not only has the advantage of low toxicity, but also is cheaper and can better control costs; (ii) The indium element possess the characteristics of both metal and non-metal, the application field of synthetic complex has the advantages of fluorescence, catalysis and magnetism, which are also reflected; (iii) In the synthesis of advanced biological materials, the indium element is low toxicity, which can enhance metabolism in a controlled degree [33]. More importantly, it should be emphasized that indium-based complexes have great fluorescence application value because of good optical stability, long fluorescent life, and high fluorescent quantum efficiency [34,35]. In particular, there has been a significant deal of research on numerous species such as iron ions, amines, and ketones, which are classical but yet limited in their common ones [36,37]. For example, Xu and co-workers had synthesized a novel highly fluorescent hybrid (Eu3+/CDs@MOF-253), which not only retained the superior optical characteristics of CDs and Eu3+ to provide dual-emission, but also had good stability in an aqueous solution [36]. Hao et al. had selected a 3D microporous compound (MIL-121) containing an uncoordinated carbonyl group, which shown excellent selectivity, fast detection time, and high sensitivity for Ag+ ions in aqueous solution [37]. In addition, the reported light-emitting in-complex sensors mainly show single function or double function detection, while the research on multi-functional sensing materials based on light-emitting complexes is rarely reported so far.

Based on the above considerations, we have synthesized a new indium-based complex by using 1,10-phenanthroline (phen) and solvothermal method, namely, [(CH3)2NH2]+·[In(phen)Cl4]− (1). The structure of the complex 1 is a zero-dimensional supramolecular structure, and the structure and fluorescence characteristics of complex 1 were thoroughly examined. The consideration of the complex and performance are as follows: (a) Indium ions with high coordination numbers can obtain complexes with diverse structures; (b) phen has the large aromatic-ring system and may provide potential supramolecular recognition sites for π–π interactions; (c) Indium and phen are two kinds of strong fluorescent materials with excellent fluorescence properties. More importantly, complex 1 exhibits excellent multi-response fluorescence sensing properties for metal cations (Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+), oxygen-containing anions (Cr2O7 2−) and toxic organic solvent (NB). Therefore, indium-based complex can be used as a kind of new fluorescent sensor with a great application potential.

Weigh 87.5 mL N,N’-dimethylformamide (DMF) and 12.5 mL concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) with a measuring cylinder, blend the concentrated HCl into the DMF, and stir on the magnetic agitator to form 1.5 mol HCl−DMF solution. A mixture of InCl3·4H2O (0.078 mmol, 17.3 mg), phen (0.029 mmol, 5.2 mg), 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (BDC) (0.028 mmol, 4.7 mg), DMF (0.75 mL), 1,4-dioxane (0.75 mL) and HCl−DMF (0.15 mL) was dispersed by ultrasound for 10 min and packed into 10 mL glass bottles. Then, put the mixture into the vacuum drying oven and keep 85 °C for three days. The combination was then progressively cooled to ambient temperature, yielding a colorless block crystal of complex 1 (Scheme 1 , yield 73 %). Calcd for C14H16Cl4InN3: C, 34.82 %; N, 8.70 %; H, 3.35 %. Found: C, 34.88 %; N, 8.68 %; H, 3.31 %. IR (KBr, cm−1): 3410 s, 2120 w, 1630s, 1570 m, 1370 m, 1230 w, 1050 w, 619 s.

All the fluorescence sensing experiments were carried out with 3 mg complex 1, ground in a mortar and air-dried, then immersed in 3 mL of 1,4-dioxane solution containing 0.1 mol L−1 of various A(NO3)n metal salts (where A = Al3+, Ba2+, Bi3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, La3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, and Sr2+; n = 2 or 3), 0.1 M aqueous solution of various K-based salts in KmB form (where B = SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CO3 2−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CrO4 2−, Cr2O7 2− and MnO4 −; m = 1 or 2) and nine common organic molecules [including acetonitrile (ACN), N,N'-dimethylacetamide (DMA), DMF, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), nitrobenzene (NB), nitromethane (NM), pyridine (Py), and tetrahydrofuran (THF), respectively. The mixed solution was then ultrasonic treated for 30 min to produce a suspension, which was measured at room temperature using a fluorescent spectrometer.

SCXRD analysis indicated that complex 1 crystallized in P − 1 space group, belonging to the triclinic system. The asymmetrical unit of the complex 1 consist of a crystallographically independent In3+ ion, one phen ligand, four Cl− anion, and one [(CH3)2NH2]+ ion. Obviously, it did not find BDC ligand in the structure. Control experiment was done without adding BDC ligand, however, it could not get any products. Therefore, it suggested that BDC acts as a “catalyst” during the crystal growth. The coordination environment of the central In3+ ion was shown in Fig. 1 a. Each In3+ ion showed a six-coordination mode with two N atoms from a phen ligand (bond length of In−N is between 2.2933(17) and 2.2966(17) Å) and four Cl− anion (bond length of In−Cl is between 2.4512(6) and 2.5622(6) Å), respectively. The N atom on the two diazole portions of the ligand was chelate-linked to one In3+ ion. The hydrogen-bonding interactions between the coordinated Cl− anions and the benzene C atoms [C(8)–H(8A)∙∙∙Cl(1)i (3.5437 Å, i 1 – x, –y, 1 – z)] were found to assemble the mononuclear In-phen into a binuclear structure (Fig. 1b). The SEM image of complex 1 and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy results presented in Fig. 1c–1f, which showed that the morphological rules of complex 1 could be clearly observed with distinct edges and high cleanliness.

Comparative structural studies of In-based complexes reveal that the ligand has a significant influence on the coordination mode of the In(III) ion during structure formation. As shown in Table 1 , by using different solvents, such as DMF and CH3CN solution, two different complexes with different coordination number of In(III) ions had been synthesized. On the other hand, when changing different ligands, the structures of the complexes also vary from 0D to 3D. As a result of the preceding explanation, the solvent and ligand might result in distinct coordination modes for the In(III) ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PXRD patterns, FTIR spectra and TGA of complex 1
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from Fig. S1, the PXRD diffraction pattern of complex 1 was consistent with the equivalent simulation pattern, indicating that the material was of good phase purity. The FTIR spectrum of the complex 1 was recorded over the frequency range of 4000–500 cm−1. As shown in Figs. S2 and S7, the peaks at 3129, 1619 and 1401 cm−1 were assigned to the CH, CN and CC stretching vibrations [41,42]. TGA had been carried out on Complex 1, and the temperature was set to rise from room temperature to 800 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min while under nitrogen protection. As can be seen from Fig. S3 that the complex 1 had two significant weightlessness, and the first weightlessness process occurred in the range of 250 °C to 300 °C, with a weight loss of about 20 %. This could be attributed to guest molecules removal of [(CH3)2NH2]+. In the range of 300 °C to 500 °C, the second weightlessness occurs, and the weightlessness in this range was about 40 %. The breakdown of the phen ligand of complex 1 was mostly responsible for the weight reduction. Lastly, the weight loss of 14.83 % observed in the range of 516 °C to 728 °C is due to the decomposition of complex 1. This indicates that the compound remaining in the sample is In2O3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and solvent stabilities of complex 1
ce:section.ce:para: We performed the following experiment to examine the thermal stability of complex 1: A tube furnace was used to heat 50 mg of complex 1 at a flow rate of 200 sccm. The heating rate was set at 10 °C/min, and the maximum temperatures were set to 150 °C, 200 °C, 250 °C, 300 °C, 350 °C, and 400 °C, respectively. Each temperature was held for one hour before being naturally cooled to room temperature. Finally, the experimental samples were recovered for PXRD measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 a, at 150 °C and 200 °C, the peak of the PXRD diffraction pattern is basically the same as that of the original complex 1, but at 250 °C, 300 °C 350 °C and 400 °C, the peak of the PXRD diffraction pattern is significantly different from that of the original complex 1. Therefore, it can be known that the complex 1 has good stability below 250 °C (excluding 300 °C) which is consistent with the result of TGA.

Moreover, we immersed the complex 1 in various organic solvents (water, ethanol, methanol, dichloromethane, acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide, 1,4-dioxane, toluene, ethyl acetate, acetone, ethylene glycol) for 24 h and changed the solvent every 8 h. Finally, the soaked complexes were filtered and dried, respectively. to explore the solvent stability of complex 1. The PXRD patterns obtained after processing closely resemble those obtained from newly manufactured crystal samples (Fig. 2b). The results reveal that the integrity and crystallinity of the frameworks are maintained, except for soaking in H2O which is significantly different from that of the original one, showing that complex 1 has strong solvent stability.

Complex 1, which is assembled with In as the metal center and phen as the organic linker, has strong fluorescence sensing potential. Based on this idea, we studied the fluorescence properties of the complex 1, phen ligand and 1,4-dioxane solution at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 3 , the emission bands of complex 1, phen ligand and 1,4-dioxane solution were 375 nm, 363 nm and 375 nm. The complex 1 exhibits red shift emission which suggested that the fluorescence property of complex 1 belongs to ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) [43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-response fluorescence sensing of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Doped ions are present in complex lattices at moderate concentrations due to differences in charge and size, and induce defects, capture electrons, and inhibit photogeneration (e−−h+) electron pairs due to valence band electron interaction (VB), which leads to enhanced sensing properties of CP by fluorescence detection of metal ions, which has piqued the interest of many researchers [45–47]. Considering its excellent solvent stability, the abilities of complex 1 to detect various metal ions in 1,4-dioxane media were studied. Fig. 4 a displayed that the fluorescence intensities of different metal ions are obviously different, and Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+ and Ni2+ ions quench the fluorescence of complex 1 to a large extent. In order to further explore the quantitative detection ability of complex 1 for quenching cations (Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+), the fluorescence intensity of complex 1 decreased significantly with the increase of the amount of quenching metal ions (Figs. 4b and S4). Fig. 4c shows the linear relationship between complex 1 and the quenching Co2+ ion. These results indicate that complex 1 can be used as a fluorescent sensor to detect Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+ and Ni2+ in 1,4-dioxane solution.

It is worth noting that Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, and Ni2+ all quenched the fluorescence intensity of complex 1 to varying degrees, prompting us to conduct titration studies to investigate the sensitivity and selectivity of complex 1 to these ions in solution (Fig. S4). With the increasing concentration of Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+ and Ni2+, the emission intensity of complex 1 continued to decrease. In Fig. 4a We found that Co2+ ions showed greater influence on the fluorescence intensity of complex 1 than Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ni2+ ions when four ions were added in equal amounts. Therefore, it is clear that the ability of complex 1 to recognize Co2+ ions is stronger than that of Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+ ions. The Stern−Volmer (S−V) Eq. (1) may be used to characterize the relationship between I0/I and concentration [48]:
(1)
I 0
/ I
= 1
+
K
S V
×
[
M
n +
]
(
n
=
2
o r
3
)
where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of complex 1 in 1,4-dioxane solution at different concentrations of Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Co2+, KSV is the quenching constant, and [Mn+] is the concentration of Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Co2+. The calculated KSV are 18.88 M−1 for Co2+, 0.08 M−1 for Fe3+, 0.01 M−1 for Cu2+, 0.02 M−1 for Ni2+ . At the same time, the limit of detection (LOD) can evaluate fluorescence probe performance with the follow Eq. (2) [49]:
(2)
L O D
=
3 σ /
K
S V
where σ is the standard deviation of fluorescence intensity of blank 5 repeats. Thus, the LOD of quenched cations are calculated as 0.01 μM for Co2+, 2.37 μM for Fe3+, 16.6 μM for Cu2+, 8.37 μM for Ni2+. Furthermore, in order to investigate the stability of the material, the complex 1 was repeatedly immersed in Co2+ solution based on 1,4-dioxane for 5 days, and fluorescence detection was carried out every day. The fluorescence test for 5 days still had a satisfactory quenching effect on the complex 1 (Fig. 4d). Meanwhile, when compared to existing complex-based sensors (Table 2 ), complex 1 has a lower LOD and consequently superior to previously reported compounds in Co2+ ion detection.

The fluorescence sensitivity of complex 1 to anion was studied. Fig. 5 a showed that the fluorescence intensity of different cations is obviously different, and the addition of Cr2O7 2− anion almost completely quenched the fluorescence intensity of complex 1. To investigate the quantitative detection capacity of complex 1 for Cr2O7 2− anion further, the fluorescence intensity of complex 1 reduced dramatically as the quantity of Cr2O7 2− anion increased (Fig. 5b). Fig. 5c shows the linear relationship between complex 1 and the quenching Cr2O7 2− anion. These results indicate that complex 1 can be served as a fluorescent sensor to detect Cr2O7 2− in 1,4-dioxane solution. Moreover, the calculated KSV is 0.01 M−1 as well as the LOD is 19.15 μM for Cr2O7 2− anion. Regeneration performance experiments were carried out on complex 1 to verify the reproducibility. The complex 1 was repeatedly soaked for 5 days, and fluorescence detection of Cr2O7 2− anion was carried out every day. The 5-day fluorescence test exhibited a reasonable quenching effect on complex 1 (Fig. S5). Furthermore, when compared to existing complex-based sensors (Table 3 ), complex 1 has a lower LOD and hence performs better than previously described compounds for sensing Cr2O7 2− anion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-response fluorescence sensing of toxic organic solvents

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a new In-based complex had been designed and synthesized. Complex 1 possessed good solution stability and thermal stability. Fluorescence sensing experiments found that complex 1 is a rare example of multi-response chemical sensors for high sensitivity and multi-response detection of metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+), oxygen-containing anion (Cr2O7 2−) and toxic organic solvent (NB), which could be considered as a universal probe of ions, anions or organic molecules. The sensing mechanism demonstrates that the synergistic impact of electron transfer between the fluorescent ligand and the FRET mechanism leads to fluorescence quenching, which is useful in a variety of applications such as analysis and sensing.

Ya-Qian Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Wan-Lin Ma: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Wen-Ze Li: Project administration, Conceptualization. Ya-Lin Lan: Software, Visualization. Yi Liu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Ya-Nan Zhao: Investigation, Software. Jian Luan: Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Fund (LJKMZ20220790, LJKMZ20220793 and LJKMZ20220795), Applied Basic Research Program of Liaoning Science and Technology Department (2023JH2/101300231).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.137380.
